coredata.dc:title: Comparative study for N and S doped carbon dots: Synthesis, characterization and applications for Fe3+ probe and cellular imaging
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Wenjing
Gong, Xiaojuan
Nan, Ming
Liu, Yang
Shuang, Shaomin
Dong, Chuan
coredata.dc:description: A facile and eco-friendly approach to prepare nitrogen(N)- and sulfur(S)-doped carbon dots (CDs) by one step microwave-assisted pyrolysis of the precursors with dl-malic acid as carbon source, ethanolamine and ethane-sulfonic acid as N and S dopants, respectively, was reported. Through the extensive investigation on morphology, chemical structures and optical properties of the carbon dots, it was found that the obtained CDs exhibited good luminescence stability, high resistance to photo bleaching and favorite solubility. Compared with undoped CDs, adding the N or S dopant could give rise to a slightly smaller particle size and a long fluorescence lifetime of CDs. Moreover, the optimal N-CDs was successfully employed as good multicolor cell imaging probes due to its fine dispersion in water, excitation-dependent emission, excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity. Besides, such N-CDs showed a wide detection range and excellent accuracy as fluorescent probe for Fe3+ ions. This probe enabled the selective detection of Fe3+ ions with a linear range of 6.0–200 μM and a limit of detection of 0.80 μM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Doped carbon dots
Fe3+ probe
Cellular imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst
xocs:meta.xocs:copyright-line: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fe3+ ion sensing capability of N-CDs and its fluorescent ink application
Toxicity of the N-CDs and its cellular imaging application

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A synthetic route of CDs using dl-malic acid as the carbon source, ethanolamine and ethanesulfonic acid as N and S dopants, respectively.
TEM and HRTEM (inset) images of the undoped-CDs (A), N-CDs (C) and S-CDs (E); and the size distribution of the undoped-CDs (B), N-CDs (D) and S-CDs (F).
XPS spectra of the undoped-CDs (A), N-CDs (C) and S-CDs (E); High-resolution XPS spectra of the C 1s of the undoped-CDs (B), N-CDs (D) and S-CDs (F).
(A) High-resolution XPS data of N 1s of N-CDs; (B) High-resolution XPS data of S 2p of S-CDs.

(A) UV–visible absorption spectra of the three kinds of CDs. (B)–(D) PL spectra of the undoped-CDs, N-CDs and S-CDs under different excitation conditions. The insets display photographs of the CDs under daylight (left) and UV irradiation (right) in aqueous solution. The concentration of CDs samples are 0.50 mg mL−1.

Representation for the possible PL mechanism of CDs. 1) Electrons excited from the ground state and trapped by the surface states; 2) excited electrons return to the ground state via a non-radiative route; 3) excited electrons return to the ground state via a radiative route.

(A) Photographs of the N-CDs solutions in the absence and presence of 50 μM Fe3+ under UV light (365 nm). (B) Comparison of PL intensities of N-CDs after adding different metal ions with the same concentration of 10 mM. (C) PL emission spectra of N-CDs upon addition of various concentrations of Fe3+ (from top to bottom, 0–200 μM). The inset displays the linear relationship between F0/F and Fe3+ concentration. (The error bars represent standard deviations based on three independent measurements.).

(A) Optical images of the rollerball pens loaded with N-CDs inks; (B) The fluorescent text written on a filter paper under a UV lamp; (C) Optical images of tremella unfold in aqueous solution of N-CDs (5.0 mg mL−1) under daylight and (D) 365 nm irradiation.

Laser scanning confocal microscopy images of HeLa cells incubated with 0.5 mg mL−1 N-CDs at 37 °C for 6 h (a–c) panels are cell images taken at λex/λem of 405/450 ± 25, 488/525 ± 25 and 559/625 ± 50 nm, respectively. (d–f) panels are the merged images of (a–c) with the corresponding bright field graphs. (g) panel is the merged images of (a) and (b). (h) panel is the merged images of (a) and (c). (i) panel is the merged images of (b) and (c). All scale bars represent 60 μm.

Laser scanning confocal microscopy images of HeLa cells incubated with 0.5 mg mL−1 N-CDs and in presence Fe3+ ions with different concentration (0.05 mM, 0.1 mM, and 0.15 mM in the culture solution). (a–d) panels are cell images taken at λex/λem of 405/450 ± 25 nm. (e–h) panels are the merged images of (a–d) with the corresponding bright field graphs, respectively. All scale bars represent 60 μm. (i) The intensities of the cells in the red circle lines were measured at λex/λem of 405/450 ± 25 nm. Data are expressed as mean ± standard deviation.

head.ce:title: Comparative study for N and S doped carbon dots: Synthesis, characterization and applications for Fe3+ probe and cellular imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile and eco-friendly approach to prepare nitrogen(N)- and sulfur(S)-doped carbon dots (CDs) by one step microwave-assisted pyrolysis of the precursors with dl-malic acid as carbon source, ethanolamine and ethane-sulfonic acid as N and S dopants, respectively, was reported. Through the extensive investigation on morphology, chemical structures and optical properties of the carbon dots, it was found that the obtained CDs exhibited good luminescence stability, high resistance to photo bleaching and favorite solubility. Compared with undoped CDs, adding the N or S dopant could give rise to a slightly smaller particle size and a long fluorescence lifetime of CDs. Moreover, the optimal N-CDs was successfully employed as good multicolor cell imaging probes due to its fine dispersion in water, excitation-dependent emission, excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity. Besides, such N-CDs showed a wide detection range and excellent accuracy as fluorescent probe for Fe3+ ions. This probe enabled the selective detection of Fe3+ ions with a linear range of 6.0–200 μM and a limit of detection of 0.80 μM.

ce:section.ce:para: Well-known fluorescent nanomaterial including quantum dots, metal nano clusters, nano crystals, etc. have been widely investigated [1]. Among a wide variety of nano sized materials, quantum dot (QD) as a forerunner has attracted great research interest [2,3], due to the unique optical properties and diverse applications, such as optical sensing, drug delivery and bio-imaging [4,5]. However, heavy metals (Such as Cd and Se) in traditional semiconductor quantum dots are the toxicity problem of concern. Carbon dots (CDs) are considered to be potential alternatives because of their remarkable advances in high resistance to photo bleaching, robust chemical inertness, low toxicity and good biocompatibility, they are popular in fluorescent probes, cellular imaging, drug carriers and so on [6–9]. Recently, a variety of methods, including laser ablation of graphite [10], electrochemical oxidation of graphite rod [11], thermal treatment of sodium citrate and ethylenediamine [12], microwaving of chitosan [13], acid treatment of sucrose solution or glucose [14] etc., have been adopted in the preparation of CDs. The synthetic CDs display unique luminescence properties such as good stability without photo-bleaching and wavelength-dependent luminescence emission. However, the emission of most CDs currently is in short-wavelength region. So many researchers commit to the development in preparing long-wavelength (e.g. red-light region) and multicolor emissive products. K. Jiang et al. [15] synthesize the CDs by using phenylenediamines as raw materials emitting bright and stable red, green and blue (RGB) colors of luminescence, under a single ultraviolet-light excitation. S. Hu et al. [16] report the synthesis and photoluminescence properties of carbon dots whose fluorescence emission wavelengths cover the entire visible spectrum (400–710 nm) which are tunable by adjustment of the reagents and synthesis conditions, and these carbon dots are excited by white light. The most astonishment behavior of CDs is the photoluminescence (PL) property, because CDs' fluorescence depends on the raw materials, which is distinguished from other semiconductor nanoparticles. CDs made from different raw materials always exhibit quite different PL phenomena. Controlling the compositions and structures of CDs is a key to understand their complicated luminescence mechanisms [17,18]. Consequently, the enthusiasm in properly designing the fluorescent CDs which aim at various of targets applications and prospecting the photoluminescence mechanisms concerning CDs keeps rising.

Particularly, by doping CDs with other elements, it is possible to adjust their compositions, structures and further improve spectral properties and applications of CDs due to alter the electronic density of CDs system [19]. Some researches involved noble metals (Au and Ag) with CDs to synthesize new type of nanomaterial. S. Mandani et al. [20] developed Au nanoparticle-carbon dot core–shell (Au@C-dot) nanocomposite by utilizing the carbon dots as reducing agents themselves and further constructed the dual colorimetric and fluorometric sensor for biothiols. However, the high cost of preparation tends to be daunting. In contrast, more active issues focused on doped CDs with other non-metallic elements by using low-cost raw materials. For instance, Krysmann et al. [21] reported pyrolysis of citric acid and ethanolamine to obtain strongly photo luminescent N-doped CDs. Jahan et al. [22] demonstrated the facile hydrothermal oxidative synthetic route to develop highly fluorescent B/N co-doped carbon nanodots. Wang et al. [23] prepared N/S/P co-doped CDs by one step hydrothermal treatment of cucumber juice. Currently, numerous reports have been presented in the nitrogen doped and nitrogen/other atom co-doped CDs. Because the nitrogen atom has a comparable atomic size and five valence electrons to bind carbon atoms, thus offering the functionalized CDs more optimized properties [19]. Beyond that, being similar to nitrogen atom, sulfur atom as a usual doping atom also captured much attention. Chandra et al. [24] synthesized and isolated sulfur doped fluorescent carbon dots for multimodal applications. However, the photoluminescence mechanisms of doped CDs with N or S element remain unclear. Thus, it is essential for comparative study about N and S doped effects on CDs luminescence for better understanding luminescence process of CDs.

On the other hand, the employ carbon-based materials as sensors for metal ions detection has caused increased attention largely. Until now, scientific papers have demonstrated the use of different carbon-based materials such as carbon nanotubes, graphene oxide (GO), nano-C60 and graphitic C3N4 (g-C3N4) nanofibers [25–29] etc. for fluorescent metal ions sensing. For example Huang et al. [30] reported the g-C3N4 nanofilms as a solid fluorescence sensor that are capable of convenient, sensitive, selective, rapid and recyclable sensing of Cu2+ and Ag+ in water and serum. Tian et al. [31] developed a highly efficient fluorosensor based on ultrathin graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) nanosheets for Cu2+. The whole detection process can be completed within 10 min with a detection limit as low as 0.5 nM. Li et al. demonstrated the use of carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) [32] and water-soluble nano-C60 [33] respectively, as fluorescent sensing platforms for highly sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ and obtained the detection limit as low as 10 nM and 500 pM exhibited excellent selectivity. Wang et al. [34] exploited a fluorescent turn-off/on method based on carbon dots as fluorescent-probes for the sensitive determination of Pb2+and pyrophosphate in an aqueous solution. Fe3+ is the most abundant essential transition metal ion in the human body and acts an important role in clinic and environment [35,36]. It is used by many proteins for oxygen-transport, electron transport, and catalyzing oxido–reductase reaction [37]. Deficiency of iron decreases oxygen delivery to cells, resulting in fatigue, poor work performance, and immunity. Also, excess amounts of Fe3+ in a living cell is connected with serious diseases [38,39]. Thus, quantitative determination of Fe3+ is of great significance. In recent years, nanomaterials have been employed as the important platform for constructing sensors toward Fe3+ detection, such as Tian et al. [25] prepared an ultrathin g-C3N4 nanofibers as a novel fluorosensor for rapid Fe3+ detection with high sensitivity and selectivity. Although these materials hold great promise in nanotechnology. However, many fluorescent nanomaterials suffer from complex synthesis routes or the involvement of toxic or expensive regents. The development of a simple, cost-effective, and green preparative strategy toward fluorescent materials for rapid Fe3+ detection with high sensitivity and selectivity is highly desired.

Herein, we report dl-malic acid as the carbon source, ethanolamine and ethane sulfonic acid as N and S dopants, respectively, for preparing carbon dots by one step microwave-assisted pyrolysis, without any additives such as salts, acids or bases. The synthesis route is facile and eco-friendly, which requires only 10 min microwave irradiation. The morphology, chemical structures and optical properties are investigated extensively. Our results show that the obtained N-CDs and S-CDs possess a uniform morphology, excellent water-solubility, fluorescence stability and good PL properties. But their chemical compositions, surface structures, QYs and sizes are quite different. Adding the N or S dopant could give a slightly smaller particle size and a long fluorescence lifetime. Doping N element would increase the QYs of CDs; on the contrary, S element would reduce it. Moreover, the N-CDs are successfully employed as the good multicolor cell imaging agents due to their excitation-dependent emission, excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity. It is potential to be utilized as a kind of fluorescent cellular imaging agent. Besides, such N-CDs have been demonstrated as effective fluorescent probes for sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+. It shows a wide detection range and excellent accuracy for Fe3+ ion. Their potential applications in intracellular Fe3+ monitoring have been explored. Our as-synthesized CDs could open up more analytical applications in bio-imaging and bio-sensing.

dl-malic acid, ethanolamine and ethane sulfonic acid were obtained from Aladdin (L.A., USA). Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM), fetal bovine serum (FBS), trypsin, Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) and 3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2, 5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) were purchased from Solarbio (Beijing, China). Other reagents were from Beijing Chemical Reagents Company (Beijing, China). All chemical reagents were used as received without any further purification. Ultrapure water (≥18.25 MΩ cm) from a Milli-Q Plus system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA) was used in all experiments.

The CDs were synthesized by a facile green route of microwave assisted pyrolysis method. For undoped-CDs, 0.2682 g (2 mM) dl-Malic acid was dissolved in 10 ml ultrapure water under ultrasonic stirring for 30 min to form a homogeneous solution. Subsequently, the clear transparent solution was put into a domestic microwave oven (700 W) and heated for 10 min. The product was dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water after cooled down to room temperature. Then the solution was filtered and dialyzed against ultrapure water through a dialysis membrane (MWCO ¼ 500–1000) for 3 days. Finally, a clear, light yellow aqueous solution containing undoped-CDs was lyophilized to collect dry undoped-CDs.

For N-CDs, 0.2682 g (2 mM) dl-Malic acid was dissolved in 10 mL ultrapure water, and then 121 μL (2 mM) Ethanolamine was added to the solution under ultrasonic stirring for 30 min to form a homogeneous solution. For S-CDs similarly, 0.2682 g (2 mM) dl-Malic acid was dissolved in 10 mL ultrapure water, and then 163 μL (2 mM) Ethane sulfonic acid was added to the solution under ultrasonic stirring for 30 min to form a homogeneous solution. The obtained two kinds of solution were treated in the same way as those for undoped-CDs.

The freeze-dried N-CDs powder was dissolved in a PBS solution (pH = 7.4) with a concentration of 1.0 mg mL−1. The selectivity for Fe3+ ion of N-CDs was confirmed by adding 0.20 mL of sixteen kinds of metal ions stock solutions (K+, Na+, Ag+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Al3+, Cr3+ and Fe3+) with a concentration of 10 mM to 1.8 mL of the N-CDs solution, respectively. Then the PL spectra were measured for recording the fluorescence intensity. To evaluate the detection range of Fe3+ in the presence of N-CDs, 1.8 mL aqueous solution of N-CDs was added in a quartz cell, followed by the addition 0.20 mL of different concentrations of Fe3+ ion, and the PL spectra were recorded after reaction for 2 min. The control sample was prepared by mixing 0.20 mL of Ultrapure water with 1.8 mL of the N-CDs solution. The reproducibility for Fe3+ ion of N-CDs was confirmed by adding 0.20 mL of Fe3+ stock solutions with a concentration of 1.0 mM to 1.8 mL of the N-CDs solution. Then the PL spectra were measured for recording the fluorescence intensity and these processes are repeated 10 times. All the samples were excited (λex) at 351 nm, and the intensities of fluorescence emission (λem) at 449 nm for each sample were recorded for comparison.

A JEM-2100 electron microscope (Tokyo, Japan) operating at 300 kV was employed to obtain the transmission electron microscopic (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images. The elemental analysis was carried out on an Elementar Analysensysteme vario EL cube elemental analyzer (Hanau, Germany). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a Bruker TensorⅡ FTIR spectrometer (Bremen, Germany). The crystal structure of three CDs were characterized by a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (λ = 0.154056 nm). X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded by a Shimadzu/Kratos AXIS ULTRA DLD photoelectron spectrometer (Kratos, Tokyo, Japan). The UV–vis absorption spectra were acquired on a TU-1901 UV–vis Spectrophotometer (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., Ltd., Beijing, China). The PL spectra were carried out on a Varian Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer. Nanosecond fluorescence lifetime experiments were performed using a FLS 920 time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) system under right-angle sample geometry. An Edinburgh EPL 405 nm ps diode laser with a repetition rate of 2 MHz (Livingston, UK) was used to excite the samples. The fluorescence was collected by a photomultiplier tube (Hamamatsu H5783p) connected to a Becker & Hickl SPC-130TCSPC board (Berlin, Germany).

For the cell cytotoxicity test, human cervical carcinoma HeLa cells were first plated on a Costar® 96-well cell culture cluster and cultured at 37 °C with 5.0% CO2 in air for 3 h to adhere cells onto the surface. The well without cells and treatment with N-CDs was taken as the zero sets. The medium was then changed with 200 μL of fresh DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS containing N-CDs and the cells were allowed to grow for another 24 and 48 h, respectively. At least six parallel samples were performed in each group. Cells not treated with N-CDs were taken as the controls. After adding 20 μL of 5.0 mg mL−1 MTT reagent into every well, the cells were further incubated for 4 h. The culture medium with MTT was removed and 150 μL of DMSO was added. The resulting mixture was shaken for ca.10 min at room temperature. The optical density (OD) of the mixture was measured at 490 nm with a SunRise microplate reader (Tecan Austria GmbH, Grödig, Austria). The cell viability was estimated as: cell viability (%) = (OD treated/OD control) × 100%, where OD control and OD treated were obtained in the absence and presence of N-CDs, respectively.

The human cervical carcinoma HeLa cells were cultured in DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS and the cells were seeded in the culture dish and cultured with 0.5 mg mL−1 N-CDs. After incubation at 37 °C for 6 h, the HeLa cells were harvest using 0.25% trypsin/0.020% EDTA, washed three times (1.0 mL each) with pH 7.4 phosphate buffered saline (PBS comprising 137 mM NaCl, 2.7 mM KCl, 8.0 mM Na2HPO4, and 2.0 mM KH2PO4) and kept in PBS for optical imaging by a Olympus FV1000 confocal microscope (Tokyo, Japan) with 20× and 40× objective. Then the addition 10 μL of Fe3+ ion with a concentration of 10.0 mM, the dynamic HeLa cell images and fluorescence intensity were monitored at various reaction times (0, 18 and 35 s). The semi-quantitative determination for Fe3+ of N-CDs on HeLa cells was confirmed by gradually adding Fe3+ stock solutions with a concentration of 10.0 mM to the culture solutions. Then the HeLa cell images and fluorescence intensity were monitored after 60 s.

In order to investigate the effects of N and S element, respectively, on the fabrication of photoluminescent CDs by the microwave assisted pyrolysis method, we selected dl-malic acid as carbon source, ethanolamine and ethanesulfonic acid as N and S dopants, respectively. The two dopants possess similar structures and all contain hydroxyl groups and the same number of carbon atoms, while the difference is the Ethanolamine with amino groups and Ethanesulfonic acid with the sulfonic groups. The synthetic route of CDs can be used to prepare different types of CDs by tuning the precursor's conditions as depicted in Fig. 1 . Various optical determinations are employed to characterize the CDs. As all the CDs products synthesized under different reaction conditions possess similar spectral characteristics, the as-prepared CDs products with 10 min microwave irradiation and 1 mol ratio of carbon source/dopants possess the highest Φs and are chosen to perform other tests, except those specially pointed out.

Fig. 2 shows transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and the size distribution of the three kinds of CDs. The TEM images clearly reveal that the doping CDs are monodisperse nanoparticles with a narrow size distribution of 2.5–5.5 nm in diameter. Their average diameters are 3.93 nm and 4.37 nm, respectively (Fig. 2D/F). Compared with undoped CDs (5.4 nm), it is found that adding the N or S dopants give a slightly smaller particle size. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images (the inset images of Fig. 2A/C/E) show that three CDs possess the crystalline structure with lattice spacing of 0.21–0.23 nm, which are very close to the (100) diffraction planes of graphite [40]. This indicates that the as-prepared CDs all possess the graphite-like structure. X-ray diffraction measurements show that these CDs have the similar broad diffraction peak at around 22.7°, 19.5°and 23.5° respectively, corresponding to an interlayer spacing of ca.0.39 nm, 0.47 nm and 0.38 nm (Fig. S1). But these values are larger than that of graphite (0.34 nm), possibly owing to the existence of oxygen-containing functional groups [41,42]. Doping N or S element will not change the crystalline structure of CDs.

The elemental analysis results of three CDs were listed in Table S1, revealing that the undoped-CDs are mainly composed of C, H, O, whereas the N-CDs contain C, H, O, N and the S-CDs contain C, H, O, S. The elemental content of the N-CDs and S-CDs differs significantly from the undoped-CDs. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) measurement was used to confirm the doping of N or S into CDs and investigate the differences in surface functionality of these CDs. The FT-IR spectra of the undoped-CDs, N-CDs and S-CDs (Fig. S2) show the absorption bands at around 3440, 2938, 1715 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibrations of O–H, C–H, CO, respectively [43–45], and the peak at 1408 cm−1 can be ascribed to the COO− group [46]. The FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs possesses an absorption peak at 1546 cm−1, indicating the formation of CC unsaturated bonds in the carbon cores which are consistent with other fluorescent N-CDs [47,48]. Moreover, other two bands at 1640 and 1400 cm−1, representing the typical stretching modes of CN and C–N, respectively, in heterocycles, are only observed in N-CDs [49,50]. The absorption peaks at 1000–1200 cm−1 are different from one another in the FT-IR spectra of the three kinds of CDs. The peaks at 1000–1200 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum of the undoped-CDs are ascribed to C–O and C–O–C bonds. The peaks in the FT-IR spectrum of the N-CDs are attributed to C–O and C–N bonds. The peaks in the FT-IR spectrum of the S-CDs are imputed to C–O, C–S and–SO3− bonds [43,51].

The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were employed to examine the surface states of the N-CDs and S-CDs. The CDs show two same apparent peaks centered at 285.0 and 531.0 eV corresponding to C 1s and O 1s (Fig. 3 A/C/E). In addition, the wide spectra of N-CDs and S-CDs (Fig. 3C and E) also show the typical peaks of N 1s (400 eV) and S 2p (168 eV), respectively. Fig. 3B, D, and F illustrate the high-resolved C1s XPS spectra of the three CDs. For undoped-CDs, the C 1s spectrum is deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.8, 286.7, 288.2 and 289.0 eV corresponding to C–C/CC, C–O, CO, and O–CO bonds, respectively [52–54]. For N-CDs, the C 1s spectrum additional peaks at 284.8, 285.9, 286.7 and 287.8 eV associated with C–C/CC, C–N, C–O and CO are observed [52,55,56]. Whereas, the C 1s spectra of S-CDs exhibits four peaks at 284.7, 285.6 and 289.5 eV corresponding to C–C/CC, C–S and O–CO groups [52], respectively. The XPS results indicate that N-CDs are mainly composed of C, O and N (the atom ratio of C/O/N is 6.72:4.51:1) and S-CDs are mainly composed of C, O as well as a limited amount of the S element (the atom ratio of C/O/S is 33.3:7.55:1). We further analyzed the high-resolution XPS data of N 1s of N-CDs and S 2p of S-CDs in detail. The N 1s spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. 4A) displays three peaks at 399.4, 399.9 and 400.4 eV, which can be ascribed to pyridinic C–N–C, pyrrolic N and graphitic N–C3 [55], respectively. The S 2p spectrum of S-CDs (Fig. 4B) can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 167.8, 168.5 and 169.6 eV, owing to –C–SOx– (x = 2, 3, 4) species, respectively [17,52,57]. Again, these confirm that N-CDs formed polyaromatic structures containing C–N and CN and the doping of S onto the surface of the S-CDs successfully. The XPS spectra of the three CDs demonstrate the presence of the same groups as indicated by the FTIR results.

UV–Vis absorption and PL spectra of these CDs were studied at room temperature. In Fig. 5 A, they all exhibit a similar absorption peak at 290–320 nm due to the n–π* transition of the CO bond [58,59]. But only N-CDs have a broad absorption peak at 245 nm, which may be ascribed to the π–π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains, should originate from the formation of multiple aromatic chromophores [58,60]. In PL spectra (Fig. 5B–D), the λem are red-shifted for the three CDs when the λex moves from 280 to 480 nm. It exhibits the typical λex-dependent PL behavior of CDs [61]. The insets of Fig. 5B–D show the optical images of three CDs under UV light (365 nm) irradiation. They emit blue colors. In addition, Fig. S3A shows that the undoped-CDs have optimal excitation and emission wavelengths at 347 nm and 432 nm, respectively. When adding the N and S dopants respectively, the excitation and emission wavelengths of CDs are: 351 nm and 449 nm for the N-CDs (Fig. S3B); 361 nm and 461 nm for S-CDs (Fig. S3C), respectively. Fig. S3D exhibits the normalization of emission wavelengths of the three CDs. The results show that adding the N or S dopants also lead to the red-shift of the maximum emission wavelengths and compared with N-CDs, S doping can bring about longer emission wavelengths.

Furthermore, the Φ
s of undoped-CDs, N-CDs and S-CDs were determined at λex/λem of 360/380–680 nm. The Φ
s are 9.86%, 15.13 and 5.51% for undoped-CDs, N-CDs and S-CDs (Fig. S4B), respectively, using quinine sulfate (Fig. S4A) as the reference. The Φ
s of N-CDs is higher than that of undoped-CDs, on the contrary, the S-CDs is lower than it. The reasons are likely that the undoped-CDs have different kinds of surface states (labeled as O-states) corresponding to a relatively wide distribution of different energy levels to generate a broad UV/Vis absorption band and, broad and excitation-dependent emission spectra. The N doping gives rise to a new kind of surface state (labeled as the N-state). Electrons trapped by the new formed surface states are able to facilitate a high yield of radiative recombination. The Φ
s of the N-CDs is higher than that of the undoped-CDs [62]. It seems that the introduced sulfur atoms would be able to eliminate the O-states, leading to that the original surface states nearly neglected in the S-CDs. Therefore, the S-CDs show a low quantum yield (Fig. 6 ). The results suggest that N doping increased the QY of CDs and the S doping reduced it.

To further investigate the origin of the CDs fluorescence, the fluorescence lifetime of the three CDs are measured and compared in Fig. S5 and Table S2. The fluorescence decay curves of them can be fitted by a double-exponential formula [44], involving the lifetimes τ1 and τ2. For N-CDs and S-CDs, the average fluorescence lifetimes are about 10.0 ns and 7.04 ns. Compared with undoped-CDs (6.27 ns), the lifetimes turn to long with the N and S doping and the average lifetime for N-CDs is the longest.

The effects of illumination time, ionic strength (in terms of the concentration of NaCl) and pH on the PL stability of the CDs were investigated. Fig. S6A depicts the photostability of the three CDs. No significant change in PL intensity was observed after 180 min exposure to xenon arc light, indicating that these CDs are highly resistant to photo bleaching. In addition, their PL intensity and spectral feature were almost unchanged with increasing concentrations of NaCl in aqueous solution (up to 2.0 M), showing high stability of the three CDs even under the high ionic-strength conditions (Fig. S6B). Fig. S6C displays the effect of pH on the PL intensity of CDs. It was obvious that their PL intensities were independent irregularly on pH values ranging from 1 to 14, since it is necessary to be used in practical applications. That also expressed doped N or S element could not affect the PL stability of the CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe3+ ion sensing capability of N-CDs and its fluorescent ink application
ce:section.ce:para:
Since the N-CDs possess high QY and excellent solubility over S-CDs, beyond that, the oxygen functional groups on the surface of N-CDs can interact with metal ions to form complex by coordination, the N-CDs provide a potential possibility to be used as a fluorescent probe for the detection of metal ions. Fe3+ is an important metal ion in life system because of its essential functions in oxygen transport, oxygen metabolism, electronic transfer and many catalytic processes [63,64]. We explored the feasibility of the N-CDs as a probe for the detection of Fe3+ in aqueous solutions. Fig. 7A shows the original N-CDs before and after quenching by Fe3+. It is obvious that the blue emission weakens after adding Fe3+ to the N-CD solution. The selectivity is an important measure of concern for evaluation of the sensing system. The effects of numerous metal ions on CDs fluorescence quenching are examined. In comparison with many common metal ions (Fig. 7B), only Fe3+ is able to quench the fluorescence of the N-CDs. The possible mechanism was shown in Fig. S7. Fe3+ ion displays the strongest PL quenching effect on N-CDs attributing to the special coordination between the Fe3+ ions and the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups of the N-CDs. A great number of Fe3+ ions become bonded to N-CDs when N-CDs is mixed with Fe3+, which undermine the stability of the luminescence properties of surface functional groups and results in a nonradiative electron transfer from the excited state of the N-CDs to Fe3+ [65]. Fig. 7C shows the fluorescent quenching of N-CDs at various concentrations of Fe3+. Upon the addition of Fe3+, a gradual decrease in PL intensity at 449 nm was observed, revealing that the PL intensity of the mixture was sensitive to Fe3+ concentration. The ΔF has good linear correlation with the Fe3+ concentrations in the range of 6.0–200 μM with a correlation coefficient r2 of 0.996 (Fig. S8). The detection limit is estimated to be 0.80 μM for Fe3+ at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 [66]. The reproducibility for Fe3+ ion of N-CDs was also evaluated by analyzing the change of the PL intensities (ΔF) of N-CDs in the absence and presence of 0.1 mM Fe3+ and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was calculated to be 4.12% based on ten independent measurements. The inset of Fig. 7C displays the linear relationship between F
0
/F and Fe3+ concentration. The quenching efficiency is fitted by the Stern–Volmer equation, F
0
/F = 1 + K sv[Q], where K sv is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant and [Q] is the Fe3+ concentration [6]. The K sv are calculated to be 2.46 × 103 L mol−1 with a correlation coefficient r
2 of 0.997. Here F
0 and F are the PL intensities of CDs at 449 nm in the absence and presence of Fe3+. Then, the fluorescence decay dynamics was measured to investigate the PL quenching mechanism of the system. The fluorescence decay curves of CDs and CDs/Fe3+ can be fitted by a double-exponential formula [41], involving the lifetimes τ1 and τ2. The average lifetime of N-CDs is 10.0 ns. After coordination with 50 μM Fe3+ ions, the lifetime decreases to 6.69 ns. The decrease in the lifetime indicates an ultrafast CDs/Fe3+ electron-transfer process and leads to dynamic quenching.

The N-CDs can be utilized for diverse applications due to their strong fluorescence effect and extremely high QY. For example, the N-CDs aqueous solutions can be used as a new type of fluorescent ink. It was noteworthy that the prepared rollerball pens loaded with the N-CDs inks exhibited bright blue luminescence (Fig. 8A) and could readily flow during writing without leakage or coagulation within the pen (Fig. 8B). Our water-soluble N-CDs inks are clear, permanent, pollution-free and washable. Therefore, it might be alternative and potential for fluorescent pens. In addition, the dry tremella could be unfolded in an aqueous solution of N-CDs (5.0 mg mL−1) and exhibited strong characteristic blue luminescence under an UV lamp (Fig. 8C and D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Toxicity of the N-CDs and its cellular imaging application
ce:section.ce:para: These encouraging results prompted us to evaluate the feasibility of N-CDs as a new fluorescent marker for living cell imaging [67–69]. For effective cellular imaging, it is required that the selected fluorescent marker possesses not only optical merits but also low cytotoxicity [67]. For the cytotoxicity of N-CDs, the viability of human cervical carcinoma HeLa cells treated with N-CDs was measured by the MTT method. As shown in Fig. S9, HeLa cells were incubated with different doses of N-CDs for 24 and 48 h, respectively. The viability of the cells remained greater than 85% even incubated with ultrahigh concentration (800 μg mL−1) of N-CDs for 48 h, demonstrating instinctively low toxicity of the N-CDs. Thus we use 0.5 mg mL−1 N-CDs to incubate with HeLa cells for cell imaging. After 6 h of incubation at 37 °C with 5% CO2, the cells are brightly illuminated and retain good morphologies under the laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM). The cells emit multicolor fluorescence under a laser of different wavelengths (Fig. 9 ). This multicolor emission shows a great advantage of the N-CDs over other tag reagents because it gives us much space to choose the wavelength for observation. That demonstrates N-CDs potential as a cellular imaging agent.

Because the N-CDs can be an effective probe for monitoring Fe3+.We assess the dynamic effect of N-CDs fluorescence in the presence of Fe3+ ions on HeLa cells, the time-dependent fluorescence images and signals at λex/λem of 559/625 ± 50 nm were acquired. Fig. S10 shows the LSCM images of the HeLa cells initially incubated with N-CDs (0.5 mg mL−1) at 37 °C for 6 h and then exposed to Fe3+ ions (0.1 mM in the culture solution) at time = 0.0 (Fig. S10a), 18 s (Fig. S10b) and 35 s (Fig. S10c), respectively. The red emissions from the cells incubated with Fe3+ diminish as the time passes. Fig. S10d displays the PL intensity of the cell red emissions in two areas after administered with Fe3+ ions. The PL intensity drops quickly after adding Fe3+ to the HeLa cells, and the entire process only needs few seconds. In addition, we also explored the semi-quantitative determination for Fe3+ of N-CDs on HeLa cells. The results show that the cells stained with N-CDs display strongest blue emissions when initially (Fig. 10a and e). However, after gradually adding Fe3+ ions in the culture solutions, the blue emission of the cell was gradually quenched (Fig. 10b–d and f–h). The intensities of cells in the red circle lines were measured at λex/λem of 405/450 ± 25 nm (Fig. 10i). It is obvious that the cells show the emission intensity weakening in presence Fe3+ ions with different concentration (0.05 mM, 0.1 mM, and 0.15 mM in the culture solution, respectively). These further demonstrate that N-CDs is an efficient fluorescent probe for monitoring the Fe3+ in live cells.

The CDs are the first synthesized by using dl-malic acid as the carbon source, ethanolamine and ethanesulfonic acid as N and S dopants, respectively, through a fast, simple and green route. The as-prepared CDs exhibit good luminescence stability, high resistance to photo bleaching and high solubility. The differences of these CDs are in their particle sizes, PL properties and QYs. In comparison with undoped CDs, the QY of N-CDs is the highest and the S-CDs' is lowest. Even though the exact mechanism is still unclear, it is reasonable to understand that the doping of nitrogen can introduce the CDs a new kind of surface state. But the doping of sulfur will lead to the original surface states neglected. Moreover, the N-CDs are successfully employed as good multicolor cell imaging agents due to their excitation-dependent emission, excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity. Beyond that, the N-CDs could be used as an effective fluorescent probe for the detection of Fe3+ ion with good selectivity and wide linear range in aqueous solution. It also would open the avenue of application in multicolor cell imaging and printing inks.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21175086, 21175087 and 21475080).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2015.09.050.
